title: Game 2: A. Khalifman vs E. Sveshnikov
fullText:
King in the Centre

Another of the main objectives of opening play is to safeguard the king. In most openings, the players advance one or more central pawns in order to develop the pieces. This has the side-effect of exposing the king and in the great majority of cases it is necessary to castle in order to avoid an early attack on the king. Castling has the beneficial side-effect of making way for the rooks to occupy the central files, which are the most likely to become open during the middlegame.

Just as with any chess guideline, there will always be exceptions to this advice. If the centre becomes blocked with pawns, so there is no easy way for the enemy to attack through the centre, then it may be possible for the king to stay in the centre. Grandmasters sometimes prepare for castling, but then delay the actual castling move, safe in the knowledge that the king can be whisked away at a moment’s notice if danger should threaten in the centre. However, it must be emphasized that these cases are the exception; in most cases, early castling is advisable. The following game is a drastic example of what may happen, even to a grandmaster, if castling is too long delayed.

A. Khalifman – E. Sveshnikov

Russian Championship, Elista 1996

Queen’s Gambit Declined

1 d4

This is one of the two most common first moves, the other being 1 e4. The view is sometimes expressed that 1 d4 is a more positional move than 1 e4. Actually, I can’t see much difference. There are many sharp openings after 1 d4, just as there are many quiet openings resulting from 1 e4.

1...d5

This symmetrical reply is one of the most popular responses.

2 c4

This is the characteristic move of the Queen’s Gambit. White intends to exchange Black’s central d-pawn for the non-central c-pawn. Then White will have two central pawns to Black’s one, which gives him chances to exercise greater control of the centre (another of the key objectives of opening play).

2...e6

Although this opening is called the Queen’s Gambit, it isn’t really a gambit at all because if Black accepts it by 2...dxc4, then he cannot maintain his extra pawn. For example, after 3 e3 b5? (3...Be6 4 Na3 regains the pawn with advantage) 4 a4 Black’s pawn-chain disintegrates and not only must he give back the pawn, but also his position is shattered. Note that 4...c6 is impossible due to 5 axb5 cxb5 6 Qf3 trapping the rook on a8. As a matter of fact, White can also regain the pawn with 3 e4 or 3 Nf3.

Rather than take on c4, it is most common to support the d5-pawn with either 2...c6 or 2...e6. Then if White plays cxd5, Black is ready with take back with a pawn, retaining a central pawn on d5.

3 Nf3

3 Nc3 is also played frequently. In many cases the two moves lead to the same position, but there are some differences since each cuts out certain options. In chess, it is desirable to remain flexible, but committal decisions are inevitable.

3...c6

Black sets up a pawn-structure typical of the Semi-Slav. This name arises because the structure with pawns on d5 and c6 is the Slav, while that with d5 and e6 is characteristic of the Orthodox Queen’s Gambit. When Black plays both ...c6 and ...e6, it is a kind of mixture of the two, which lends some logic to the name ‘Semi-Slav’. This pawn-structure is very popular in modern grandmaster chess. It is solid, since the important central d5-pawn is reinforced, while at the same time Black can often develop his pieces comfortably by ...Nf6, ...Nbd7, ...Bd6 and ...0-0. That only leaves his c8-bishop to be deployed, but Black hopes to solve this by either ...dxc4, followed by ...b5 and ...Bb7, or by ...b6.

4 g3

4 Nc3 would lead into the main lines of the Semi-Slav. Khalifman’s interesting idea is based on the point that Black often plays ...dxc4 followed by ...b5 in the Semi-Slav. By placing his bishop on the long diagonal, he is ready to exploit the weakening of that diagonal resulting from ...dxc4. The risk is that this is a genuine gambit. We saw above that White can regain the pawn after 2...dxc4, because the bishop on f1 helps White break up Black’s b5-c4 pawn-chain. However, when the bishop is on g2, White cannot regain the pawn by force.

4...Nf6

Black could have taken the pawn at once by 4...dxc4 5 Bg2 b5, but he decides to develop a piece first.

5 Bg2

Some players have chosen to defend the c4-pawn by 5 Nbd2 or 5 Qc2, but Khalifman persists with offering a gambit.

5...dxc4

Sveshnikov takes up the challenge. The alternative was to continue his development without accepting the sacrifice (for example by 5...Nbd7). While Sveshnikov understandably wants to exploit the negative side of White’s plan, he now comes under a dangerous attack and it would have been more prudent to decline the offer.

6 0-0

The immediate 6 Ne5 is also possible. It is true that Black then has the disruptive 6...Bb4+, but after 7 Nc3 (not 7 Bd2? in view of 7...Qxd4, however) 7...Nd5 8 Bd2 White again has good compensation for the pawn.

6...b5

Black has no hope of retaining the pawn on c4 without this move and if he is forced to play it, then it is most flexible to play it straight away. After 6...Nbd7 7 a4 b5, for example, 8 axb5 cxb5 9 Ng5! Nd5 10 e4 Nc7 11 e5 Rb8 12 Qh5 g6 13 Qf3 gives White a near-decisive attack. If Black simply allows White to regain the pawn on c4 then he will stand at a disadvantage. The whole point of his opening play was to offer the d5-pawn firm support. To give up the strong point at d5 without the compensation of an extra pawn would be totally inconsistent and would allow White a free hand in the centre.

7 a4

Threatening 8 axb5 followed by a discovered attack on the long diagonal, much as in the line given in the previous note.

7...Bb7

Black counters the threat by reinforcing the long diagonal.

This is a good moment to stop and assess the situation. Positionally speaking, White has a good game since he has an extra central pawn and a lead in development. Black, on the other hand, has a pawn more. Judging the relative merits of material and positional advantages is always difficult, and this is not a clear-cut case. However, one important point is clear: one of the main elements of White’s compensation is the fact that Black is still two moves away from castling. It follows that White must operate with direct threats so far as possible, to keep Black off-balance and deny him the two tempi he needs to safeguard his king. Moreover, White must try to open lines. At present, there is quite a lot of wood between White’s forces and the enemy king; as lines are opened, White’s attacking possibilities will increase.

8 Ne5

Creating the direct threat of 9 axb5, and bringing two pieces to bear on the weak c6-pawn. If at any stage Black shields this pawn by ...Nd5, then White will gain the free tempo e4 for his attack.

8...Qb6

Black defends his bishop, so as to answer axb5 by ...cxb5. 8...a6 is another idea, but after 9 axb5 axb5 10 Rxa8 Bxa8 11 Nc3, White brings out a piece while at the same time threatening 12 Nxb5, so once again Black is unable to develop his kingside.

9 b3!

This very strong move was played for the first time in the current game. Hitherto, White had preferred 9 e4, but the text-move keeps Black more off-balance, as he must counter the threat of 10 bxc4.

9...cxb3

Forced, as otherwise White regains his pawn while keeping his attack.

10 Qxb3

Now White has no immediately devastating threat, but Black will have to be ready to answer White’s attack on the b5-pawn after 11 Na3.

10...Nbd7?

Black decides that the priority is to get rid of the dominating knight on e5, but he cannot afford to waste any time in preparing kingside castling. A better line was 10...Be7 11 Na3 0-0 (returning the extra pawn is best; after 11...b4 12 Nac4 Qxd4 13 Bb2 White has an enormous attack for the two pawns) 12 axb5 cxb5 13 Bxb7 Qxb7 14 Qxb5. Here White still has a slight advantage, since his e5-knight is active while Black has yet to move his b8-knight. It is possible that Sveshnikov rejected 10...Be7 because he did not relish the prospect of defending this inferior position, but if so he made a common chess mistake: in attempting to avoid a small disadvantage, he ends up with a large one!

The greedy 10...Qxd4 is probably also better than the text-move, even though Black’s position hangs on a knife-edge. After 11 Bb2 Qb6 12 axb5 cxb5 13 Bd4! Qxd4 (13...Bd5 loses material to 14 Qxd5!) 14 Bxb7 Qxa1 (14...Qxe5 15 Ra5 a6 16 Bxa8 Bd6 17 Bf3 is good for White; the open files are ideal for the rooks and Black cannot develop his b8-knight) 15 Qxb5+ Nbd7 16 Nxd7 Nxd7 17 Bxa8 Qe5 18 Qa4 Qc7 Black can just about keep his position intact thanks to a neat tactical point: 19 Rd1 Bd6 20 Na3 0-0! (20...Ke7 21 Nb5 Qc5 22 Nxd6 Rxa8 23 Ne4 Qc7 24 Qa3+ Ke8 25 Nd6+ wins) 21 Nb5 Qb8 22 Rxd6 a6! 23 Nc7 (23 Rxa6? Nc5) 23...Nb6! and Black is able to defend.

11 Be3!

Now White’s attack really starts to gather momentum. The immediate threat is the central breakthrough 12 d5.

11...c5?!

Opening the position usually plays into the hands of the better-developed player. Black could have tried 11...Nd5 12 Nxd7 Kxd7, attempting to set up some sort of blockade in the centre; for example, 13 a5 Qa6 14 Bd2 f5 and White cannot easily force through e4. However, there cannot be many players who would like to take on a position like this, extra pawn or no extra pawn. Black’s queen is sidelined on a6, and his king is floating around in the centre.

12 Nxd7

This exchange is the preliminary to a central breakthrough.

12...Nxd7

13 d5!

An excellent move based on an accurate assessment of the position. White could have achieved a safe advantage by 13 dxc5 Bxc5 14 a5 Qc7 15 Bxb7 Qxb7 16 Bxc5 Nxc5 17 Qc3, followed by Qxg7, but in this case Black would still have defensive chances. Khalifman, however, perceives that his position is so strong that he should not be content with a modest advantage, but should be playing for more. By opening lines in the centre, he creates avenues of attack against the black king. Moreover, by threatening 14 dxe6, he once again gives Black no time to bring his f8-bishop out. The fact that this move costs White a second pawn is of little relevance.

13...bxa4

White wins after 13...exd5 14 Nc3! d4 15 a5! Qe6 (15...Qa6 loses to 16 Nxb5) 16 Qxe6+ fxe6 17 Bxb7 Rb8 18 Bc6; for example, 18...dxe3 19 Rfd1 Rd8 20 Rxd7 Rxd7 21 Rd1. Nor is 13...c4 satisfactory in view of 14 Qxb5 Qxb5 15 axb5 Bxd5 16 Bxd5 exd5 17 Nc3 Nf6 18 b6 with a large advantage for White.

14 Qxa4

Black has solved the problem of White’s threats to his b5-pawn, but at the cost of setting up a pin along the a4-e8 diagonal, which will in the end prove decisive. Moreover, the opening of the b-file will inevitably present White with a free tempo when he plays a rook to b1.

14...exd5

14...Bxd5 fails to help Black. After 15 Nc3! Bxg2 16 Kxg2 Qb7+ (if Black omits this check, then he will have to answer Rab1 by ...Qc7, giving White another free tempo after Bf4) 17 Kg1 Rd8 18 Rab1 Qc7 (after 18...Qc8 19 Bf4, followed by Rb8, Black has to part with his queen and, ironically, he will still be unable to castle) 19 Rfd1 Rc8 (19...Be7 20 Bf4 e5 21 Nd5 Qd6 22 Rb7! is decisive) 20 Nb5 Qc6 21 Qa5 Qb6 22 Qa1 White has a winning position.

15 Nc3

White is now two pawns down, but his lead in development has increased. All his pieces are now in play, whereas Black is still two moves away from castling kingside. In addition, White has opened various lines: the b-file and a4-e8 diagonal are already open, and if the d5-pawn falls then the d-file will also be open. These lines provide ample opportunity for White’s pieces to converge on Black’s helpless king, which is held in the centre by a combination of poor development and the pinned d7-knight.

15...d4

There is no defence. 15...Bc6 16 Nxd5! Bxa4 17 Nxb6 axb6 18 Bxa8 gives White a material advantage and a large lead in development, while 15...Qc6 (15...Qb4 is also met by 16 Bxd5) 16 Bxd5 Qxa4 17 Rxa4 Bxd5 18 Nxd5 Bd6 19 Ra6! Bb8 20 Rd1 completely ties Black up (20...0-0? 21 Ne7+).

Perhaps 15...Qe6 is the only real alternative to the text-move. Then White wins by thematically smashing open lines: 16 Nxd5! Bxd5 (or 16...Bd6 17 Nc7+ Bxc7 18 Bxb7 and now after 18...Rb8 19 Bc6 or 18...Rd8 19 Bxc5 Black is again prevented from castling) 17 Bxd5 Qxd5 18 Rfd1 Qe6 19 Rxd7! (the various open lines all come into play in this combination – the d-file, the e-file and the various diagonals) 19...Qxd7 20 Qe4+ Be7 21 Qxa8+ Qd8 (or 21...Bd8 22 Bxc5 and – dare I say it again? – Black cannot castle!) 22 Rxa7 Qxa8 (22...0-0 loses to 23 Qxd8 Bxd8 24 Bxc5 Re8 25 Ra8 followed by Bb6) 23 Rxa8+ Bd8 24 Bxc5, followed by Bb6, and White wins the pawn ending after exchanging off all the pieces on d8 (in K+Ï endings with all the pawns on the same side, an extra pawn is usually enough to win with 2 vs 1, and almost always wins with 3 vs 2 or 4 vs 3).

16 Nd5!

Not the careless 16 Rfb1?, when 16...Bxg2! 17 Rxb6 axb6 18 Qd1 Rxa1 19 Qxa1 Bc6 turns the tables.

16...Bxd5

White also wins after 16...Qc6 17 Qxc6 Bxc6 18 Nc7+ Kd8 19 Nxa8 Bxg2 20 Kxg2 dxe3 21 Rfd1 or 16...Qd8 17 Bf4 Rc8 18 Nf6+ Qxf6 19 Bxb7 Rd8 20 Bc7.

17 Bxd5

White’s bishop exercises unopposed control over the light squares.

17...Rd8

Black supports his pinned knight, but he will never have time for ...Be7 and ...0-0.

18 Bf4

18...Qf6

After 18...Bd6 (or 18...Qb4 19 Qa2! and f7 collapses, while if 18...Be7, then 19 Bc6 Qb4 20 Qxa7 and Black is doomed by his pinned knight) 19 Rfb1 Qc7 20 Bg5 f6 (20...Rb8 21 Rxb8+ Qxb8 22 Bxf7+ Kxf7 23 Qxd7+ Kf8 24 Rxa7 is winning for White) 21 Rb7 Qxb7 (21...Qc8 22 Bd2, followed by Ba5, is painful) 22 Bxb7 fxg5 Black has a miscellaneous collection of bits and pieces for the queen, but White’s powerful initiative decides the game quickly: 23 Qc6 Ke7 (23...Be7 24 Rxa7) 24 Rxa7 Bb8 25 Qe4+ and Black collapses.

19 Qb5

The simplest. White clears the way for the a1-rook to penetrate to a6 or a7.

19...Bd6

The bishop finally moves, only to land immediately in a fatal pin. However, 19...Be7 20 Rxa7 Qb6 21 Qxb6 Nxb6 22 Bc6+ Kf8 23 Bc7 results in heavy loss of material, while 19...Qb6 20 Qc4 Qg6 21 Rxa7 Be7 22 Qb5 puts Black’s position under intolerable pressure.

20 Ra6

This second pin decides the game at once.

20...Rb8

As good or bad as any other move.

21 Bb7

White maintains both pins.

21...Bxf4

Giving up the queen for only a rook is equivalent to resignation.

22 Rxf6

22...gxf6

23 gxf4

23...f5

Black is still gripped by the pin on his knight.

24 Qc6

Threatening 25 Bc8.

24...Kd8

Unpinning the knight.

25 Qd5

Pinning it again. Now 26 Bc6 is the threat.

25...Kc7

26 Bc6

26...1-0

The c5-pawn falls, and mate is not far off.

This was a very dynamic win by Khalifman. After offering a pawn, he kept Sveshnikov under pressure with a constant barrage of threats. When Black turned down the last chance to bring his king into safety (as early as move 10) he was immediately in considerable difficulties. Khalifman realized the importance of opening lines and was prepared to offer further material to strengthen his attack. In the later stages, Black’s problems were compounded by a crippling weakness on the light squares.

The lessons here are:

1) Castling is desirable in the large majority of games.

2) A king left in the centre can easily become the target of an attack.

3) When pursuing such an attack, keep the defender off-balance.

4) The key to many attacks is the opening of lines towards the enemy king.

There is no defence. 15...Bc6 16 Nxd5! Bxa4 17 Nxb6 axb6 18 Bxa8 gives White a material advantage and a large lead in development, while 15...Qc6 (15...Qb4 is also met by 16 Bxd5) 16 Bxd5 Qxa4 17 Rxa4 Bxd5 18 Nxd5 Bd6 19 Ra6! Bb8 20 Rd1 completely ties Black up (20...0-0? 21 Ne7+).

Perhaps 15...Qe6 is the only real alternative to the text-move. Then White wins by thematically smashing open lines: 16 Nxd5! Bxd5 (or 16...Bd6 17 Nc7+ Bxc7 18 Bxb7 and now after 18...Rb8 19 Bc6 or 18...Rd8 19 Bxc5 Black is again prevented from castling) 17 Bxd5 Qxd5 18 Rfd1 Qe6 19 Rxd7! (the various open lines all come into play in this combination – the d-file, the e-file and the various diagonals) 19...Qxd7 20 Qe4+ Be7 21 Qxa8+ Qd8 (or 21...Bd8 22 Bxc5 and – dare I say it again? – Black cannot castle!) 22 Rxa7 Qxa8 (22...0-0 loses to 23 Qxd8 Bxd8 24 Bxc5 Re8 25 Ra8 followed by Bb6) 23 Rxa8+ Bd8 24 Bxc5, followed by Bb6, and White wins the pawn ending after exchanging off all the pieces on d8 (in K+Ï endings with all the pawns on the same side, an extra pawn is usually enough to win with 2 vs 1, and almost always wins with 3 vs 2 or 4 vs 3).

White to play
Not the careless 16 Rfb1?, when 16...Bxg2! 17 Rxb6 axb6 18 Qd1 Rxa1 19 Qxa1 Bc6 turns the tables.
White also wins after 16...Qc6 17 Qxc6 Bxc6 18 Nc7+ Kd8 19 Nxa8 Bxg2 20 Kxg2 dxe3 21 Rfd1 or 16...Qd8 17 Bf4 Rc8 18 Nf6+ Qxf6 19 Bxb7 Rd8 20 Bc7.
White’s bishop exercises unopposed control over the light squares.
Black supports his pinned knight, but he will never have time for ...Be7 and ...0-0.
Black to play

After 18...Bd6 (or 18...Qb4 19 Qa2! and f7 collapses, while if 18...Be7, then 19 Bc6 Qb4 20 Qxa7 and Black is doomed by his pinned knight) 19 Rfb1 Qc7 20 Bg5 f6 (20...Rb8 21 Rxb8+ Qxb8 22 Bxf7+ Kxf7 23 Qxd7+ Kf8 24 Rxa7 is winning for White) 21 Rb7 Qxb7 (21...Qc8 22 Bd2, followed by Ba5, is painful) 22 Bxb7 fxg5 Black has a miscellaneous collection of bits and pieces for the queen, but White’s powerful initiative decides the game quickly: 23 Qc6 Ke7 (23...Be7 24 Rxa7) 24 Rxa7 Bb8 25 Qe4+ and Black collapses.
The simplest. White clears the way for the a1-rook to penetrate to a6 or a7.

The bishop finally moves, only to land immediately in a fatal pin. However, 19...Be7 20 Rxa7 Qb6 21 Qxb6 Nxb6 22 Bc6+ Kf8 23 Bc7 results in heavy loss of material, while 19...Qb6 20 Qc4 Qg6 21 Rxa7 Be7 22 Qb5 puts Black’s position under intolerable pressure.
This second pin decides the game at once.
As good or bad as any other move.

Black to play

White maintains both pins.
Giving up the queen for only a rook is equivalent to resignation.
Black is still gripped by the pin on his knight.
Threatening 25 Bc8.
Unpinning the knight.
Pinning it again. Now 26 Bc6 is the threat.

26...1-0

The c5-pawn falls, and mate is not far off.

This was a very dynamic win by Khalifman. After offering a pawn, he kept Sveshnikov under pressure with a constant barrage of threats. When Black turned down the last chance to bring his king into safety (as early as move 10) he was immediately in considerable difficulties. Khalifman realized the importance of opening lines and was prepared to offer further material to strengthen his attack. In the later stages, Black’s problems were compounded by a crippling weakness on the light squares.

The lessons here are:

1) Castling is desirable in the large majority of games.

2) A king left in the centre can easily become the target of an attack.

3) When pursuing such an attack, keep the defender off-balance.

4) The key to many attacks is the opening of lines towards the enemy king.
